# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Application Logic

This section walks through **worked analytical examples** for the most exam-relevant quantitative and case-based problems in this module.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: Applying PDCA to a Quality Problem

### Case Setup
A Kerala-based spice-processing firm has seen its *customer complaints* rise from 12/month (Jan–Mar) to 47/month (Oct–Dec). The Quality Council commissions a PDCA-based improvement project.

### Step-by-Step PDCA Application

**STEP 1 — PLAN: Define the Problem**

$$ \text{Complaint increase} = \frac{47 - 12}{12} \times 100\% = 291.67\% $$

A 292% increase over 9 months is alarming. The SMART objective:

> "Reduce monthly customer complaints from 47 to ≤ 15 by 30 June, with zero repeat complaints related to foreign-matter contamination."

**STEP 2 — PLAN: Observe the Process**

The team uses a *SIPOC* (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers) map to understand the end-to-end flow from raw chili procurement to packaging.

**STEP 3 — PLAN: Analyze Root Cause**

A *Pareto analysis* of complaint categories shows:

| Complaint Type | Frequency | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|
| Foreign matter | 22 | 46.8% |
| Weight shortfall | 11 | 70.2% |
| Packaging damage | 8 | 87.2% |
| Label errors | 4 | 95.7% |
| Misc | 2 | 100% |

The team drills into foreign matter using the **5-Why** technique:

- *Why 1:* Foreign matter in packs. → Why?
- *Why 2:* Cleaning stage missed items. → Why?
- *Why 3:* Sortation conveyor too fast. → Why?
- *Why 4:* Set at 2.5 m/s (spec: 1.5 m/s). → Why?
- *Why 5:* Setting was changed by a worker to boost throughput, no SOP enforced.

**Root cause identified: SOP non-compliance at sortation.**

**STEP 4 — PLAN: Develop Countermeasures**

- Reset conveyor to 1.5 m/s per SOP
- Install visual speed gauge
- Train operators on the 5-Why and quality cost
- Add periodic supervisor audit

**STEP 5 — DO: Pilot Implementation**

Pilot on Line 2 for 4 weeks. Data collected daily.

**STEP 6 — CHECK: Verify Effects**

$$ \text{Complaints (Line 2)}: 47/\text{month} \rightarrow 14/\text{month} $$

$$ \text{Reduction} = \frac{47 - 14}{47} \times 100\% = 70.2\% $$

Statistically significant at $p < 0.05$ using a chi-square test of complaint counts.

**STEP 7 — ACT: Standardize**

- SOP revised with clear speed setting
- Line supervisors trained
- New KPI added to dashboards
- Recognition given to pilot team

**STEP 8 — ACT: Next Opportunity**

Move on to weight-shortfall issue (next Pareto item). The cycle continues.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** A case study like this, requiring you to *apply PDCA* to a real situation, is the most common question in this module. Master this template.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Framework: Supplier Rating Computation

### Problem
A firm evaluates 3 suppliers on 4 criteria. Weights and ratings (out of 10) are:

| Criterion | Weight |
|---|---|
| Quality (PPM) | 0.40 |
| Delivery (OTD) | 0.25 |
| Cost (TCO) | 0.20 |
| Service | 0.15 |

Ratings:

| Criterion | Supplier A | Supplier B | Supplier C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Delivery | 7 | 9 | 7 |
| Cost | 6 | 7 | 9 |
| Service | 8 | 8 | 7 |

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Total weighted score formula:**

$$ S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \times r_i $$

**Verification of weights:**

$$ \sum w_i = 0.40 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark $$

**Supplier A calculation:**

$$ S_A = (0.40 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.15 \times 8) $$

$$ S_A = 3.60 + 1.75 + 1.20 + 1.20 $$

$$ S_A = 7.75 $$

**Supplier B calculation:**

$$ S_B = (0.40 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 8) $$

$$ S_B = 2.80 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 1.20 $$

$$ S_B = 7.65 $$

**Supplier C calculation:**

$$ S_C = (0.40 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7) $$

$$ S_C = 3.20 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 1.05 $$

$$ S_C = 7.80 $$

### Decision Table

| Supplier | Total Score | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| **C** | **7.80** | **1** |
| A | 7.75 | 2 |
| B | 7.65 | 3 |

**Interpretation:**
- Supplier C is the highest-scoring supplier, primarily driven by *cost* performance.
- However, Supplier A has the best *quality*, which carries the highest weight.
- **Strategic recommendation:** Use C for *cost-driven commodity items* and A for *quality-critical safety items*. B is dropped or developed further.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** Note that the *winner* depends on weights. If you change $w_{quality}$ to 0.60, A wins. The exercise illustrates that *weights encode strategy*.

## 3.3 Analytical Framework: Strategic Quality Planning via Hoshin Kanri

### Step-by-Step Hoshin Kanri Cascade (Adapted for KTU)

**Step 1 — Vision (CEO)**
> "Achieve world-class food safety by 2027."

**Step 2 — Breakthrough Objectives (Quality Council)**

- Reduce customer-reported safety incidents by 80%
- Achieve FSSC 22000 certification across all plants
- Implement supplier audit program covering 100% of Tier-1 suppliers

**Step 3 — Annual Objectives (Divisions)**

| Division | Objective | Owner | Timeline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plant Ops | Implement HACCP in all lines | Plant Head | Q2 |
| Supply Chain | Audit 100% Tier-1 suppliers | SCM Head | Q3 |
| R&D | Reformulate top-10 products for cleaner labels | R&D Head | Q4 |
| HR | Train 100% workforce in food safety | HR Head | Q1 |

**Step 4 — Catchball (Negotiation between levels)**

Managers negotiate targets, resources, and timelines. *This is the heart of Hoshin Kanri* — it is not top-down, not bottom-up, but *diagonal* through dialogue.

**Step 5 — Action Plans (Individual contributors)**

Specific projects, KPIs, due dates, budgets, and review cadences.

**Step 6 — Monthly Reviews & Quarterly Audits**

Using a *Hoshin Kanri X-matrix* to track alignment.

### The X-Matrix (Conceptual Table)

| ↑ Objectives → | Breakthrough 1 | Breakthrough 2 | Breakthrough 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Measure** | Incident count | Cert % | Audit % |
| **Target** | ≤ 20/yr | 100% | 100% |
| **Owner** | Plant Head | Quality Director | SCM Head |
| **Linked Initiative** | HACCP | FSSC 22000 | Supplier audits |
| **Linkage** | Links to Breakthrough 1 | Links to 2 | Links to 3 |

## 3.4 Analytical Framework: Implementing Kaizen in a 5-Day Event

### Step-by-Step Kaizen Event Plan

**Day 1 — PLAN**
- Train team in 5S, PDCA, problem-solving tools
- Baseline current state (cycle time, defects, distance walked)
- Define scope and objectives
- *Output:* Baseline metrics, charter

**Day 2 — DO (Phase 1)**
- Apply 5S (sort, set-in-order, shine, standardize, sustain)
- Re-layout workstation
- Implement visual management
- *Output:* Before-photos, layout drawing

**Day 3 — DO (Phase 2)**
- Pilot new workflow
- Train operators on standard work
- Identify remaining wastes (TIMWOODS — Transport, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects, Skills-underutilization)
- *Output:* Standard Work document, training records

**Day 4 — CHECK**
- Measure new cycle time and defect rate
- Compare with baseline
- Document deviations, refine standards
- *Output:* Before/after charts, revised SOPs

**Day 5 — ACT**
- Standardize new method
- Present results to management
- Recognize team (plaques, photos, certificates)
- Identify next Kaizen theme
- *Output:* Handover document, celebration

### Quantitative Illustration of Kaizen Impact

**Baseline (Day 1):**
- Cycle time: 8.4 minutes/unit
- Defect rate: 6.2%
- Walking distance: 45 m/shift

**Post-Kaizen (Day 4):**
- Cycle time: 5.1 minutes/unit
- Defect rate: 1.4%
- Walking distance: 22 m/shift

**Improvements:**

$$ \text{Cycle-time reduction} = \frac{8.4 - 5.1}{8.4} \times 100\% = 39.3\% $$

$$ \text{Defect reduction} = \frac{6.2 - 1.4}{6.2} \times 100\% = 77.4\% $$

$$ \text{Motion reduction} = \frac{45 - 22}{45} \times 100\% = 51.1\% $$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Quantify the improvement. Numbers speak louder than adjectives in a 14-mark answer.

## 3.5 Analytical Framework: Diagnosing a Failed TQM Implementation

A KTU case-study type question often presents a firm where TQM "did not work." Your job is to *diagnose* using a change-management lens.

### Step 1 — Map the Symptoms to the Change-Management Stage

| Symptom | Likely Stage Skipped | Kotter Step Missed |
|---|---|---|
| "Employees sabotage the new SOP" | Unfreeze | 1. Urgency / 4. Communicate |
| "We trained them but nothing changed" | Change | 5. Empower / 6. Wins |
| "Worked for a year, then faded" | Refreeze | 7. Build on / 8. Anchor |

### Step 2 — Apply the 5 Whys to the *Root Cause of Failure*

- *Why 1:* TQM failed. → Why?
- *Why 2:* People reverted to old habits. → Why?
- *Why 3:* No reinforcement mechanism. → Why?
- *Why 4:* Leaders stopped reviewing quality metrics. → Why?
- *Why 5:* The CEO got transferred; the successor didn't believe in TQM.

**Root cause:** *Leadership discontinuity.* The cure is *Kotter Step 8* — anchor TQM in *culture*, not in *a single person*.

### Step 3 — Recommend a Recovery Plan

1. Re-educate the new leadership (sensitivity training, TQM benefits data)
2. Re-launch with a new urgency campaign
3. Pick a *low-hanging* quality issue, win fast
4. Form a fresh Quality Council with the new CEO as chair
5. Cascade the vision through Hoshin Kanri
6. Anchor in SOPs, KPIs, recognition, and onboarding

## 3.6 Analytical Framework: Quality Circle Problem-Solving (Worked Example)

**Problem:** A QC in a bakery identifies "frequent cake-collapse after baking" as a theme.

**Standard QC-Story walkthrough:**

| # | Step | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theme | Cake collapse (defect rate 9.4%) |
| 2 | Define | 9.4% cakes collapse; mostly 1-kg vanilla; mostly afternoon shift |
| 3 | Cause analysis | Fishbone: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Environment |
| 4 | Data | 1-kg vanilla, afternoon shift, oven #3 → 7.2% of all collapses |
| 5 | Analysis | 5-Why on oven #3 → faulty thermostat (deviation 12°C) |
| 6 | Countermeasure | Calibrate thermostat daily, install alarm at ±5°C |
| 7 | Implement | Trained bakers; SOP updated |
| 8 | Effect | Defect dropped to 1.1% — savings ₹3.2 lakh/month |
| 9 | Standardize | Updated SOP, training module, daily log |
| 10 | Future | Next theme: "lumpy batter in chocolate cake" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** The QC Story format is a *frequently tested 14-mark question*. Memorize the 10 steps and the *verbs* (Identify, Define, Analyze, etc.).

## PART B — 14-Mark Full Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has TWO sub-parts (typically 7 + 7 marks). Internal choice means you may be offered Q11 OR Q12, both of 14 marks, with sub-parts.

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the **role of leadership** in implementing TQM in an organization. Discuss **any three leadership styles** suitable for TQM environments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **process of Strategic Quality Planning**. How does **Hoshin Kanri** support the deployment of quality strategy from top management to the shop floor? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Q.A (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Leadership is the most critical enabler of TQM. According to Deming's 14 Points, *constancy of purpose* and *adopt the new philosophy* require committed, visionary, and involved leaders.

**Role of Leadership in TQM (3 marks):**

1. **Sets the Quality Vision and Direction** — Leaders articulate why quality matters and where the organization must reach.
2. **Allocates Resources** — Budget, training time, and tools for improvement projects.
3. **Builds and Sustains the Quality Culture** — Through role modeling, recognition, and consistent messages.
4. **Establishes the Quality Council** — Provides the governance structure for TQM.
5. **Drives Customer Focus** — Ensures VOC is captured and acted upon.
6. **Champions Continuous Improvement** — Personally reviews PDCA outcomes and removes barriers.

**Three Leadership Styles Suitable for TQM (3 marks):**

1. **Transformational Leadership** — Inspires followers to embrace a vision beyond self-interest. *Best fit* for TQM because it mobilizes people around quality and innovation. Bass identified four components: idealized influence, inspirational motivation, intellectual stimulation, individualized consideration.
2. **Servant Leadership** — Puts employees first, focuses on their growth and well-being. Fits TQM's empowerment philosophy (Greenleaf).
3. **Participative/Democratic Leadership** — Involves the team in decision-making. Aligns with employee involvement and quality circles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *list* styles without explaining *why* they fit TQM. The 'why' is what earns the 3 marks. Always tie the style back to a TQM principle (e.g., empowerment, involvement, vision).

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Effective TQM leaders use a situational blend, with transformational as the dominant style.

---

#### Model Answer — Q.A (b)

**Introduction to Strategic Quality Planning (1.5 marks):**
Strategic Quality Planning is the systematic integration of quality into the long-term organizational strategy. It transforms quality from a *function* to a *strategy*.

**Process Steps (3 marks):**

1. **Customer needs identification (VOC)** — Surveys, interviews, complaints analysis.
2. **Current state assessment** — Quality audits, process capability studies, SWOT.
3. **Gap analysis** — Where are we vs. where we should be.
4. **Goal setting** — SMART quality objectives at corporate, division, and unit levels.
5. **Action plan development** — Projects, owners, milestones, budgets.
6. **Resource allocation** — People, tools, training, capital.
7. **Performance measurement** — KPIs, dashboards, reviews.
8. **Review and refine** — Annual strategic quality review.

**Hoshin Kanri (Japanese Policy Deployment) (2 marks):**

Hoshin Kanri is a *systematic* method of strategic deployment that ensures top-management quality goals cascade to the frontline and that frontline feedback flows back up. The process is called *catchball* — ideas are tossed between levels, refined, and committed to.

**How Hoshin Kanri Supports Strategic Quality Deployment (0.5 mark):**
- **Alignment** — Every level knows the *same* breakthrough objectives.
- **Engagement** — Catchball ensures ownership, not compliance.
- **Visibility** — The X-matrix shows linkages across plans.
- **Adaptation** — Annual review and recalibration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students describe Hoshin Kanri as merely "top-down goal setting." The examiner expects the *catchball* concept and the *X-matrix*. Both are non-negotiable for full marks.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** What is a **Quality Circle**? Explain its **structure, problem-solving process, and benefits** to an organization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **concept of Supplier Partnership**. Compare it with **traditional purchasing**. Explain the **supplier selection and rating process** with a suitable numerical example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Q.B (a)

**Definition (1 mark):**
A Quality Circle is a small group of 6–12 frontline employees from the same work area who meet regularly to identify, analyze, and solve quality and productivity problems in their own workplace. Pioneered by **Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa** in Japan in 1962.

**Structure (2 marks):**

| Element | Description |
|---|---|
| **Members** | 6–12 voluntary frontline workers |
| **Leader** | Senior member elected by the group |
| **Facilitator** | Middle manager (coaches, doesn't control) |
| **Frequency** | ~1 hour/week, paid time |
| **Hierarchy of Circles** | Department → Plant → Corporate QCs |

**Problem-Solving Process (3 marks):** (Use the 10-step QC Story)

1. Theme Selection — choose a specific, measurable problem.
2. Problem Definition — quantify baseline.
3. Cause Analysis — 5-Why, fishbone, Pareto.
4. Data Collection — gather before-improvement data.
5. Data Analysis — charts, statistics.
6. Countermeasure Development — generate, select.
7. Implementation — pilot or full rollout.
8. Effect Confirmation — compare before/after.
9. Standardization — SOPs, controls.
10. Future Plans — next theme.

**Benefits (1 mark):**
- Improved quality and productivity
- Employee engagement and morale
- Cost savings from grassroots ideas
- Skill development in problem-solving
- Bottom-up innovation pipeline

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often describe QCs as "informal discussion groups." This is *incorrect*. They are *structured*, with formal problem-solving methodology, regular meetings, and management presentations. The *structured* nature is the answer-key's marker.

---

#### Model Answer — Q.B (b)

**Concept of Supplier Partnership (1.5 marks):**
A Supplier Partnership is a long-term, win-win, trust-based alliance between a buyer and key supplier(s) focused on joint value creation, shared risk, and continuous improvement. It moves away from the price-driven, adversarial model.

**Comparison: Supplier Partnership vs. Traditional Purchasing (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Traditional | Partnership |
|---|---|---|
| Duration | Short-term, transactional | Long-term (3–10 years) |
| Supplier count | Many (price-driven) | Few (capability-driven) |
| Information | Limited | Open-book, integrated |
| Quality approach | Inspection | Process capability, prevention |
| Risk | Pushed to supplier | Shared |
| Improvement | Supplier's job | Joint Kaizen |

**Supplier Selection Process (1.5 marks):**

1. Identify the need
2. Define selection criteria and weights
3. Identify potential suppliers
4. Evaluate using weighted scoring
5. Audit top contenders
6. Onboard the chosen supplier

**Supplier Rating (Numerical Example) (2.5 marks):**

A firm rates 3 suppliers on 4 criteria with the following weights: Quality (0.40), Delivery (0.25), Cost (0.20), Service (0.15). Ratings (out of 10) are:

| Criterion | Weight | Supplier X | Supplier Y | Supplier Z |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 0.40 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Delivery | 0.25 | 7 | 9 | 7 |
| Cost | 0.20 | 6 | 7 | 9 |
| Service | 0.15 | 8 | 8 | 7 |

Total score formula:

$$ S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \times r_i $$

**Supplier X:**

$$ S_X = 0.40(9) + 0.25(7) + 0.20(6) + 0.15(8) = 3.60 + 1.75 + 1.20 + 1.20 = 7.75 $$

**Supplier Y:**

$$ S_Y = 0.40(7) + 0.25(9) + 0.20(7) + 0.15(8) = 2.80 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 1.20 = 7.65 $$

**Supplier Z:**

$$ S_Z = 0.40(8) + 0.25(7) + 0.20(9) + 0.15(7) = 3.20 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 1.05 = 7.80 $$

**Decision:** Supplier Z ranks highest (7.80), followed by X (7.75) and Y (7.65). However, X has the best quality score, and since *quality carries the highest weight (0.40)*, the firm may strategically split-volume-purchase between Z (cost-driven items) and X (quality-critical items).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often skip the *weight verification* step. Always confirm $\sum w_i = 1$ before calculation. Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing this. Also, the *interpretation* of the score (winner analysis) is worth 1 mark, not just the arithmetic.

| 1 | Confusing *involvement* with *empowerment* | 1–2 | Involvement = asking; Empowerment = authorizing |
| 2 | Listing Kotter's 8 steps without *explanation* | 2–3 | Each step needs a *what* and a *why* |
| 3 | Drawing PDCA as a *one-time* cycle | 1 | PDCA is *iterative* — must show the loop back |
| 4 | Describing QCs as "casual discussions" | 2 | QCs are *structured* with formal methodology |
| 5 | Forgetting $\sum w_i = 1$ in supplier rating | 1 | Always verify weights sum to 1 |
| 6 | Misdefining strategic planning as "annual goal setting" | 2 | It is *long-term* (3–5 years) and *integrated* |
| 7 | Skipping the *why* when naming leadership styles | 2 | Tie each style to a TQM principle |
| 8 | Conflating *partnership* with *long-term contract* | 1 | Partnership = trust + joint value, not just duration |
| 9 | Not showing *baseline vs. after* numbers in Kaizen | 2 | Always quantify improvement |
| 10 | Missing *cultural anchoring* (Kotter Step 8) in change mgt. | 2 | Embedding in culture is what *sustains* TQM |

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Save This Page!**

### Leadership & Strategy
- TQM starts at the **top**; leadership is the *first* enabler
- **Transformational + Servant** leadership = TQM best fit
- **Strategic Quality Planning** = Vision → Mission → Policy → Objectives → Plans → Measures
- **Hoshin Kanri** = Japanese policy deployment with *catchball* and *X-matrix*
- **Quality Council** = CEO-led cross-functional steering committee

### People & Teams
- **Employee Involvement** = participation in decisions
- **Empowerment** = authority to act (4 elements: authority, information, resources, accountability)
- **Motivation theories** = Maslow, Herzberg (2-factor), McGregor (X/Y), Vroom (Expectancy: M = E × I × V)
- **Tannenbaum-Schmidt ladder** = 7 levels from telling to empowering
- **Tuckman's stages** = Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning
- **Quality Circles** = 6–12 frontline, weekly, 10-step QC Story, voluntary
- **Recognition** = non-monetary often beats monetary; must be *immediate* and *specific*
- **Performance Appraisal** = 360-degree, balanced scorecard, development-oriented

### Improvement Tools
- **PDCA** = 4-stage iterative cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act); the *operating system* of TQM
- **Kaizen** = "change for the better" — small, daily, everyone; *operating philosophy*
- **Innovation/Kaikaku** = big, radical — needed alongside Kaizen
- **5S** = Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke — *foundation* of Kaizen
- **Kaizen Event** = 5-day blitz (Plan-Do-Check-Act structure)
- **CPI** = continuous process improvement — never-ending incremental upgrade

### Suppliers
- **Supplier Partnership** = long-term, win-win, trust-based alliance
- **Partnering** = the *operational mechanism* of the alliance (joint planning, open books, co-located engineers)
- **Supplier Selection** = multi-criteria; **weighted score method** is the KTU favorite
- **Supplier Rating** = ongoing scorecard; **PPM, OTD, TCO, Service** are standard metrics
- Formula: $S = \sum w_i \times r_i$, with $\sum w_i = 1$

### Change Management
- **Lewin** = Unfreeze → Change → Refreeze
- **Kotter** = 8 steps (urgency → culture anchor)
- **Bridges** = Ending → Neutral Zone → New Beginning
- **Top 3 failure causes**: poor leadership, weak change mgmt, no short-term wins
- **TQM sustainability = culture embedding**, not a project

### Key One-Liners for Last-Minute Recall
- *"Quality is not inspected in, it is built in"* — Kaoru Ishikawa
- *"It is not necessary to change. Survival is not mandatory"* — W. Edwards Deming
- *"Every employee is a quality inspector"* — TQM principle
- *"Kaizen is a marathon, not a sprint"*
- *"Without satisfied employees, you cannot have satisfied customers"* — Karl Albrecht
- *"Supplier is not a vendor; it is a partner"*
- *"The only constant is improvement"* — PDCA essence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overviews

### 1. Benchmarking – Concept & Intuition

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
Benchmarking is the continuous, systematic process of measuring one's own products, services, and processes against the *best-in-class* performers within or outside the industry, in order to identify performance gaps and implement improvements that lead to superior performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Phrasing:** Benchmarking is **NOT** spying on competitors — it is a *legally ethical, documented, structured learning process* where the goal is to **adapt** (not copy) superior practices.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "School Topper" Method:**
Imagine a student named Riya who consistently scores 60/100 in mathematics. Instead of giving up, she finds the class topper (who scores 95), observes *how* they study (timing, materials, methods), and **adapts** those techniques to her routine. Within 6 months, Riya's score rises to 85. Riya didn't *copy* the topper's paper — she **learned from the best and improved herself**. That is exactly what Benchmarking does for organizations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of Benchmarking:** The term "Benchmark" originates from **surveyors** who chiseled reference marks (bench marks) into stone to measure elevations. The benchmarking concept in TQM was pioneered by **Rank Xerox (1979)** when it compared itself to Canon and Japanese rivals.

**Core Benchmarked Metrics (Industry-Standard):**
- **Cycle Time** (seconds)
- **Defect Rate (DPMO)** — Defects Per Million Opportunities
- **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** in **INR/USD**
- **On-Time Delivery (OTD) %**
- **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)**
- **First Pass Yield (FPY) %**

---

### 2. Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) – Concept & Intuition

**Formal Definition:**
FMEA is a **proactive, systematic, team-based analytical technique** used to identify potential failure modes of a product, process, or service, evaluate their severity, occurrence, and detectability, and prioritize corrective actions to prevent failures **before** they reach the customer.

**The "Pre-Flight Checklist" Analogy:**
Before every flight, pilots run through a pre-flight checklist — they don't wait for the engine to fail! They ask: *"What could fail? How bad would it be? How often? Can we detect it before takeoff?"* — and act on the answers. FMEA is exactly the same: a **pre-flight check for engineering and process design**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Origin:** FMEA was first formally developed by the **U.S. Military (MIL-STD-1629, 1949)** and later adapted for automotive (QS-9000), aerospace (AS9100), and medical devices (ISO 14971).

**The Risk Priority Number (RPN) Formula:**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

Where:
- **S** = Severity (1–10): How serious is the failure to the customer?
- **O** = Occurrence (1–10): How frequently does the failure occur?
- **D** = Detection (1–10): How hard is it to detect the failure *before* it reaches the customer?

> [!WARNING]
> **Note on New AIAG-VDA (2019):** The traditional RPN has been **replaced** by an **Action Priority (AP)** matrix (H, M, L) in the updated FMEA standard, but **KTU exams (2024) still use the classic RPN formula**. We cover both for completeness.

---

### 3. Lean Manufacturing – Concept & Intuition

**Formal Definition:**
Lean Manufacturing is a systematic methodology (originating from the **Toyota Production System – TPS**) focused on the *elimination of waste (Muda)* in all forms, while delivering exactly what the customer needs, when they need it, in the quantity they need, without any waste.

**The "Refrigerator" Analogy:**
Open any household refrigerator. You'll find: 3-month-old leftovers, expired sauces, vegetables hidden behind milk cartons that rot unseen, multiple half-used bottles of the same condiment. **Lean** says: *Clear the fridge, only keep what you'll eat this week, arrange so the oldest is in front, and stop buying duplicates.* The 'waste' was hiding in plain sight. Lean identifies and removes that waste in factories.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 7 Wastes (TIMWOOD + 1) — Industry Standard:**
> 1. **T**ransportation (unnecessary movement of materials)
> 2. **I**nventory (excess stock / WIP)
> 3. **M**otion (unnecessary movement of people)
> 4. **W**aiting (idle time of machines/people)
> 5. **O**verproduction (making more than needed)
> 6. **O**ver-processing (doing more work than required)
> 7. **D**efects (rework, scrap, warranty)
> 8. **S**kills/Talent (underutilized human potential — added in modern Lean literature)

---

### 4. Fishbone Diagram (Ishikawa Diagram) – Concept & Intuition

**Formal Definition:**
The Fishbone Diagram, also known as the **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** or **Ishikawa Diagram**, is a structured brainstorming tool developed by **Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa (1968)** that visually categorizes the potential root causes of a problem (the "effect") into major cause categories, helping teams perform **root-cause analysis**.

**The "Detective's Board" Analogy:**
Think of a detective movie — a corkboard with a photo of the victim at the center, connected by red strings to suspects, weapons, and motives. Each thread is a *category of clues*. The Fishbone diagram is the quality detective's corkboard: the **"effect"** (the problem) is the head of the fish, and the **bones** are categories of possible causes. The team then "pins" specific causes onto each bone.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 6M Framework (Ishikawa's Original Categories):**
> - **Man (Workforce)** — skills, training, fatigue
> - **Machine (Equipment)** — condition, capability, maintenance
> - **Material (Input)** — quality, specification, supplier
> - **Method (Process)** — procedures, work instructions, sequence
> - **Measurement (Inspection)** — gauges, calibration, accuracy
> - **Mother Nature (Environment)** — temperature, humidity, dust, light
>
> **In Service/IT**, the 6M is replaced by the **4S Framework**: *Surroundings, Suppliers, Systems, Skills*.

---

### 5. Design of Experiments (DOE) – Concept & Intuition

**Formal Definition:**
Design of Experiments (DOE) is a **statistical methodology** (pioneered by **Sir Ronald A. Fisher, 1920s, Rothamsted Agricultural Station, UK**) in which deliberate, controlled changes are made to input variables (factors) of a process, in order to observe and quantify the corresponding changes in output variables (responses), thereby identifying the **optimal operating conditions** and the **dominant factors** affecting quality.

**The "Recipe Testing" Analogy:**
A chef wants the perfect chocolate cake. She suspects that **baking temperature (°C)**, **baking time (min)**, and **cocoa type (3 brands)** all matter. Instead of randomly trying recipes, she systematically bakes cakes:
- 180°C × 20 min × Brand A
- 180°C × 25 min × Brand A
- 200°C × 25 min × Brand A
- … and so on through every combination.
She **records** taste scores. From the data, she concludes: *"Temperature has the biggest effect; Brand A is best; 200°C × 22 min is optimal."* — This is **DOE**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Core DOE Terms (must memorize for exams):**
> - **Factor (X):** The input variable we vary (e.g., temperature).
> - **Level:** The specific value a factor takes (e.g., 180°C, 200°C).
> - **Response (Y):** The output variable measured (e.g., cake taste score).
> - **Replicate:** A repeated run under the same factor levels (to estimate random error).
> - **Main Effect:** The average effect of one factor on the response, ignoring others.
> - **Interaction Effect:** The effect of one factor *depends* on the level of another factor.
> - **Experimental Unit:** The smallest entity to which a treatment is independently applied.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 1. Benchmarking — Theory, Types, and Process

#### 1.1 Types of Benchmarking (KTU High-Yield)

| **Type** | **What is Compared** | **Pros** | **Cons** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Internal** | Operations across branches/shifts of the *same* company | Easy data access, low cost | Limited fresh ideas | Comparing 5 factories of Tata Motors |
| **Competitive** | Direct competitor's product/process | Highly relevant to market | Data is hard to obtain (legally) | Maruti vs Hyundai |
| **Functional (Generic)** | Best-in-class *function* (not necessarily same industry) | Cross-industry innovation, novel ideas | Lessons may not transfer | Hospital benchmarks hotel check-in for patient admission |
| **Strategic (Process)** | Core business processes end-to-end | Strategic-level insights | Time-consuming | Apple vs Samsung R&D process |
| **Performance** | Performance metrics (KPIs) directly | Quantitative clarity | Doesn't reveal *how* they did it | DPMO comparison across plants |

#### 1.2 The 5-Phase Benchmarking Process (Robert C. Camp, KTU Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robert C. Camp's 5-Phase Model (1989)** is the **most frequently tested** benchmarking process in KTU exams.

**Phase 1 — Plan:**
- Identify the process to benchmark (e.g., "Order Fulfillment").
- Define the metric (e.g., "Order delivery time").
- Identify the benchmarking partner.

**Phase 2 — Analyze:**
- Collect data on *own* process (As-Is).
- Collect data on partner's process.
- Compute the **performance gap** = $\Delta = P_{\text{partner}} - P_{\text{self}}$.

**Phase 3 — Integrate:**
- Communicate findings to leadership.
- Set *realistic* improvement targets (not unachievable ideals).

**Phase 4 — Action:**
- Develop an action plan (with timeline, budget, owner).
- Implement changes incrementally.

**Phase 5 — Maturity:**
- Make benchmarking a **continuous** habit.
- Recalibrate targets as you catch up.

**Mathematical Representation of the Performance Gap:**

$$\text{Performance Gap} = P_{\text{partner}} - P_{\text{self}}$$

**Percentage Improvement Required:**

$$\text{\% Improvement} = \frac{P_{\text{partner}} - P_{\text{self}}}{P_{\text{self}}} \times 100$$

#### 1.3 Reasons to Benchmark (KTU Top 7)

1. **Identify performance gaps** objectively.
2. **Learn best practices** without R&D cost.
3. **Set realistic performance targets** (vs arbitrary goals).
4. **Achieve competitive parity or leadership.**
5. **Foster a culture of continuous improvement.**
6. **Reduce cost of poor quality (COPQ).**
7. **Increase customer satisfaction and retention.**

#### 1.4 Applications

- **Manufacturing:** Cycle-time reduction, scrap reduction, OEE improvement.
- **Service Sector:** Hospital patient wait-time benchmarking, hotel check-in time.
- **IT Sector:** Help-desk resolution time, software defect density, Cloud SLA performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Service vs IT Benchmarking Examples:**
> - **Service:** Airlines benchmarking on-time performance (IndiGo vs SpiceJet).
> - **IT:** Infosys benchmarking its Agile sprint velocity against TCS/Cognizant.

---

### 2. FMEA — Deep Theory

#### 2.1 The Three Critical Ratings (Each 1–10)

| **Rating (S/O/D)** | **Score 1 (Best)** | **Score 5 (Moderate)** | **Score 10 (Worst)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Severity (S)** | No impact | Major loss of function | Safety hazard, injury |
| **Occurrence (O)** | 1 in 1,000,000 | 1 in 2,000 | ≥ 1 in 2 (almost always) |
| **Detection (D)** | Almost certain to detect | Moderate chance | Cannot detect / no control |

#### 2.2 RPN Thresholds (KTU Standard)

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

| **RPN Value** | **Action Level** |
|---|---|
| **< 100** | Acceptable — monitor |
| **100 – 200** | Reduce through controls |
| **> 200** | Mandatory corrective action |

#### 2.3 Types of FMEA

| **Type** | **Scope** | **Used In** |
|---|---|---|
| **System FMEA** | Whole system-level | Concept phase, aerospace, defense |
| **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** | Product design | Automotive (AIAG-VDA), mechanical products |
| **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** | Manufacturing process | Assembly lines, machining, casting |

#### 2.4 Stages of FMEA (8-Stage Standard, KTU 2024)

1. **Assemble a cross-functional team** (Design, Manufacturing, Quality, Service).
2. **Define the scope & boundaries** (product, process, customer use).
3. **Identify potential failure modes** for each function.
4. **Assess S, O, D scores** for each failure mode.
5. **Compute RPN** and rank failures.
6. **Prioritize** (RPN > threshold first).
7. **Recommend corrective actions** to lower RPN.
8. **Document & reassess** RPN after action.

---

### 3. Integration of Lean Manufacturing Principles with TQM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Question (14-Mark Favourite):** *"Compare TQM and Lean and discuss how they can be integrated."* — This appears **almost every university exam cycle.**

#### 3.1 The Five Lean Principles (Womack & Jones, 1996)

1. **Value** — Define value from the *customer's* perspective.
2. **Value Stream** — Map every step of the process; eliminate non-value-adding steps.
3. **Flow** — Make remaining steps flow without interruption.
4. **Pull** — Produce only when the customer pulls (downstream triggers upstream).
5. **Perfection** — Continuously improve toward perfection.

#### 3.2 TQM vs Lean — Similarities & Differences

| **Dimension** | **TQM** | **Lean** |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Customer satisfaction, process quality | Waste elimination, flow efficiency |
| **Origin** | Deming, Juran, Crosby (1950s USA) | Toyota Production System (1950s Japan) |
| **Approach** | Broad — covers entire organization | Narrow — focuses on process flow |
| **Key Tools** | SPC, FMEA, QFD, Pareto | VSM, 5S, Kanban, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke |
| **Metric** | Defects per Million (DPMO), σ-level | Lead time, Takt time, OEE |
| **Mindset** | "Do it right the first time" | "Eliminate what doesn't add value" |

#### 3.3 Integration — How Lean & TQM Complement Each Other

- **TQM = "What" to improve** (Customer-driven quality goals)
- **Lean = "How" to improve** (Waste-eliminating tools)
- **Combined = Lean Six Sigma (LSS):** Uses **Lean's speed** + **Six Sigma's data-driven DMAIC rigor**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Synergy Framework:**
> - TQM provides the **philosophy & culture** (Kaizen mindset, customer obsession).
> - Lean provides the **operational tools** (VSM, 5S, Kanban, Poka-Yoke).
> - The integration is sometimes called **"Lean TQM"** or **"Total Lean Management."**

---

### 4. Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram — Construction Rules

#### 4.1 The 6M Categories (Manufacturing) — Memorize for KTU

| **M-Category** | **Example Causes** |
|---|---|
| **Man** | Operator skill gap, fatigue, training deficiency |
| **Machine** | Equipment wear, calibration drift, breakdown |
| **Material** | Wrong grade, contamination, supplier defect |
| **Method** | Outdated SOP, wrong sequence, missing step |
| **Measurement** | Gauge error, sampling error, no SPC |
| **Mother Nature** | Humidity, temperature, dust, lighting |

#### 4.2 4S Categories (Service/IT)

**Surroundings · Suppliers · Systems · Skills**

#### 4.3 Construction Steps (KTU Process)

1. **Define the effect** (right side, fish head).
2. **Draw the main spine** (the long arrow to the head).
3. **Add the 6M category branches** as angled bones.
4. **Brainstorm** specific causes for each category.
5. **Use 5-Why analysis** to drill down to root cause.

---

### 5. Design of Experiments (DOE) — Theoretical Foundation

#### 5.1 Why DOE? (The Power of Structured Testing)

Without DOE, varying **k factors at 2 levels** one-at-a-time (OFAT) requires $2k$ runs and **misses interactions**.

DOE's full-factorial design requires $2^k$ runs but **captures all main effects AND interactions**.

| **# of Factors (k)** | **OFAT Runs (One-Factor-At-A-Time)** | **$2^k$ Full-Factorial Runs** |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 4 | 4 (same) |
| 3 | 6 | 8 |
| 4 | 8 | 16 |
| 5 | 10 | 32 |

#### 5.2 Types of DOE (KTU High-Yield)

| **Type** | **Runs Required** | **Use** | **Catches Interactions?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Factorial $2^k$** | $2^k$ | All main + all interactions | Yes, all |
| **Fractional Factorial $2^{k-p}$** | $2^{k-p}$ | Screening, large k | Only some (assumed few active) |
| **Taguchi (Orthogonal Arrays, e.g., L4, L9, L27)** | Small (L9 = 9 runs for 4 factors at 3 levels) | Robust design, industrial | Pre-defined resolution |
| **Response Surface (RSM, e.g., CCD, Box-Behnken)** | 15–50+ | Optimization after screening | Yes (quadratic) |
| **One-Factor-At-A-Time (OFAT)** | $2k$ | Quick checks | **No** |

#### 5.3 The $2^k$ Factorial Design — Key Equations

For a **2-factor, 2-level** design (a $2^2$ design) with factors $A$ and $B$ at levels $(-1, +1)$:

The linear model is:

$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 A + \beta_2 B + \beta_{12} AB + \varepsilon$$

Where:
- $\beta_0$ = grand mean
- $\beta_1$ = main effect of A
- $\beta_2$ = main effect of B
- $\beta_{12}$ = interaction effect of A×B
- $\varepsilon$ = random error

**Main Effect Calculation (for factor A, signed in coded units):**

$$\beta_1 = \frac{\text{Sum of }Y \text{ at }A=+1 - \text{Sum of }Y \text{ at }A=-1}{2^{k-1} \cdot n}$$

For a **single replicate** ($n=1$) of $2^2$ design, with runs $(A,B) = (-,-), (+,-), (-,+), (+,+)$ giving responses $y_1, y_2, y_3, y_4$:

$$\beta_1 = \frac{-y_1 + y_2 - y_3 + y_4}{4}$$

$$\beta_2 = \frac{-y_1 - y_2 + y_3 + y_4}{4}$$

$$\beta_{12} = \frac{+y_1 - y_2 - y_3 + y_4}{4}$$

#### 5.4 Steps in DOE (KTU 5-Step Process)

1. **State the problem** and identify the response $Y$.
2. **Identify factors and levels** (use fishbone + Pareto to narrow down).
3. **Select the design** (full / fractional / Taguchi).
4. **Conduct the experiment** (randomize run order to avoid bias).
5. **Analyze** (ANOVA, main effects plot, interaction plot) and **confirm** with confirmation runs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students confuse "factors" with "levels." Quick memory aid: **"Factor is the ingredient, Level is the amount of that ingredient."**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 1. Worked Example: Benchmarking Performance Gap Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-style 3-mark / short):**
A company benchmarks its order delivery time against a partner. The company delivers in **120 hours**, and the partner delivers in **72 hours**. Calculate:
(i) The performance gap in hours.
(ii) The percentage improvement required.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**
- $P_{\text{self}} = 120$ hours
- $P_{\text{partner}} = 72$ hours

**Step 2 — Apply the performance gap formula.**

$$\text{Performance Gap} = P_{\text{partner}} - P_{\text{self}} = 72 - 120 = -48 \text{ hours}$$

The negative sign indicates the company is **48 hours slower** than the partner.

**Step 3 — Apply the percentage improvement formula.**

$$\% \text{ Improvement} = \frac{P_{\text{partner}} - P_{\text{self}}}{P_{\text{self}}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Improvement} = \frac{72 - 120}{120} \times 100 = \frac{-48}{120} \times 100 = -40\%$$

**Result:** The company must improve its order delivery time by **40%** to match the benchmarking partner.

---

### 2. Worked Example: FMEA RPN Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU 7-mark style):**
In a CNC machining process, consider the failure mode **"Tool wear causes dimensional deviation."** The quality team has assessed:
- Severity $S = 8$ (major dimensional non-conformance)
- Occurrence $O = 5$ (occurs 1 in 2000 cycles)
- Detection $D = 4$ (caught by SPC chart with some delay)

Compute the RPN and state the recommended action.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the RPN formula.**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$RPN = 8 \times 5 \times 4$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product.**

$$RPN = 160$$

**Step 4 — Compare to the threshold.**
- $RPN = 160$ lies in the range **100 – 200** → **"Reduce through controls"** action level.

**Step 5 — Recommend corrective action.**
- Introduce **automatic tool-offset compensation** (reduces O).
- Add **in-process probing** (reduces D).
- Add **tool-life management software** (reduces O further).
- After action, RPN must be **recalculated** and should ideally drop below 100.

---

### 3. Worked Example: $2^2$ Factorial DOE — Effect Estimation

**Problem Statement (KTU 14-mark style):**
A process engineer studies the effect of two factors on surface roughness ($R_a$ in $\mu m$):
- Factor **A** = Cutting Speed (low = $-1$: 100 m/min, high = $+1$: 200 m/min)
- Factor **B** = Feed Rate (low = $-1$: 0.1 mm/rev, high = $+1$: 0.2 mm/rev)

The four runs (in coded units) yielded the following $R_a$ values:

| **Run** | **A** | **B** | **$R_a$ ($\mu m$)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-1$ | $-1$ | 1.2 |
| 2 | $+1$ | $-1$ | 0.8 |
| 3 | $-1$ | $+1$ | 1.6 |
| 4 | $+1$ | $+1$ | 0.4 |

**Required:** Calculate the main effect of A, the main effect of B, and the interaction effect AB. State which factor is dominant and whether the interaction is significant.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the effect formulas for a $2^2$ design with single replicate.**

The $2^2$ design has a $2 \times 2$ sign matrix where each column's sign for a run is given by the standard Yates pattern:

| **Run** | **I** | **A** | **B** | **AB** | **$Y$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $+1$ | $-1$ | $-1$ | $+1$ | $y_1 = 1.2$ |
| 2 | $+1$ | $+1$ | $-1$ | $-1$ | $y_2 = 0.8$ |
| 3 | $+1$ | $-1$ | $+1$ | $-1$ | $y_3 = 1.6$ |
| 4 | $+1$ | $+1$ | $+1$ | $+1$ | $y_4 = 0.4$ |

**Step 2 — Calculate the grand mean $\beta_0$ (intercept).**

$$\beta_0 = \frac{y_1 + y_2 + y_3 + y_4}{4} = \frac{1.2 + 0.8 + 1.6 + 0.4}{4} = \frac{4.0}{4} = 1.0 \text{ } \mu m$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the main effect of A (Cutting Speed).**

Multiply each $Y$ by the sign in column A and sum, then divide by 4:

$$\beta_1 = \frac{(-1)(1.2) + (+1)(0.8) + (-1)(1.6) + (+1)(0.4)}{4}$$

$$\beta_1 = \frac{-1.2 + 0.8 - 1.6 + 0.4}{4} = \frac{-1.6}{4} = -0.4 \text{ } \mu m$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the main effect of B (Feed Rate).**

$$\beta_2 = \frac{(-1)(1.2) + (-1)(0.8) + (+1)(1.6) + (+1)(0.4)}{4}$$

$$\beta_2 = \frac{-1.2 - 0.8 + 1.6 + 0.4}{4} = \frac{0.0}{4} = 0.0 \text{ } \mu m$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the interaction effect AB.**

$$\beta_{12} = \frac{(+1)(1.2) + (-1)(0.8) + (-1)(1.6) + (+1)(0.4)}{4}$$

$$\beta_{12} = \frac{1.2 - 0.8 - 1.6 + 0.4}{4} = \frac{-0.8}{4} = -0.2 \text{ } \mu m$$

**Step 6 — Write the fitted model.**

$$Y = 1.0 - 0.4 A + 0.0 B - 0.2 AB + \varepsilon$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the results.**

| **Term** | **Magnitude** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|
| $\beta_0 = 1.0$ | — | Average roughness is 1.0 μm |
| $\beta_1 = -0.4$ | **Largest** | Cutting Speed has the **strongest** effect. Moving from low to high speed **reduces roughness by 0.4 μm** |
| $\beta_2 = 0.0$ | Zero | Feed Rate has **no main effect** in this data |
| $\beta_{12} = -0.2$ | Moderate | A×B interaction exists: the effect of speed **depends** on feed rate (worth investigating further) |

**Step 8 — Practical conclusion.**
The optimal setting is **High Speed ($+1$) and High Feed ($+1$)**, giving the lowest observed $R_a = 0.4$ μm. To confirm statistical significance, an **ANOVA table with replication** would be required (out of KTU scope for the 14-mark problem, but mention it for full marks).

---

### 4. Worked Example: Fishbone Diagram — 5-Why Analysis

**Problem Statement (KTU 7-mark style):**
A factory observes an **increase in weld defects** (effect). Using the **Fishbone (6M) framework** and **5-Why analysis**, drill down to the root cause.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construct the 6M framework (table form since diagram drawing is verbal here):**

| **6M Category** | **Brainstormed Cause** | **5-Why Drilling** |
|---|---|---|
| **Man** | Welder skipped training | Why? → Training schedule conflict. Why? → New welder hired last week. **Root cause: No onboarding process.** |
| **Machine** | Welding torch voltage fluctuating | Why? → Power supply unstable. Why? → No UPS on shop floor. **Root cause: Missing power backup.** |
| **Material** | Electrode coating varies batch-to-batch | Why? → Multiple suppliers. Why? → No supplier audit. **Root cause: No Approved Vendor List enforcement.** |
| **Method** | No standard weld parameter sheet at station | Why? → SOP outdated. Why? → No document control. **Root cause: Missing document control system.** |
| **Measurement** | Visual inspection only, no NDT | Why? → NDT equipment unavailable. Why? → Budget not allocated. **Root cause: Inspection under-investment.** |
| **Mother Nature** | Workshop humidity > 80% in monsoon | Why? → No climate control. Why? → Plant not designed for humidity. **Root cause: Layout / HVAC gap.** |

**Step 2 — Rank the root causes by impact (Pareto prioritization).**

**Step 3 — Recommend top 3 corrective actions:**
1. Implement document control + update WPS/PQR (Method).
2. Add NDT (UT / RT) at sampling stations (Measurement).
3. Enforce single-source qualified electrodes with COC (Material).

**Step 4 — Verify** by re-running the FMEA on the updated process.

---

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define Benchmarking. List any three reasons why an organization should adopt benchmarking.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Benchmarking is the continuous, systematic process of measuring an organization's products, services, and processes against those of best-in-class performers, in order to identify performance gaps and implement improvements.

**Three Reasons:**
1. **Identify performance gaps** objectively rather than relying on intuition.
2. **Set realistic, data-driven improvement targets** based on proven performance levels.
3. **Learn industry best practices** without incurring the full R&D cost of original innovation.
4. *(Optional)* Foster a culture of continuous improvement and increase customer satisfaction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA? Write the formula and explain the three components.**

**Model Answer:**

**RPN Definition:** RPN is a numerical score used in FMEA to rank the relative risk of each identified failure mode, helping teams prioritize corrective actions.

**Formula:**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

**Three Components:**
- **Severity (S):** A 1–10 rating of how seriously the failure would impact the customer.
- **Occurrence (O):** A 1–10 rating of how frequently the failure mode is likely to occur.
- **Detection (D):** A 1–10 rating of the difficulty of detecting the failure *before* it reaches the customer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Detection** with **Inspection**. Detection is the *probability of detection* — a high score = **hard to detect** (bad), a low score = **easy to detect** (good). This is a common 1-mark loss in KTU valuation.

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Variant]**

**(a)** Define Benchmarking. Explain the **Robert C. Camp 5-Phase Benchmarking Process** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **applications of Benchmarking in the Service Sector and the IT Sector**, with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Definition & 5-Phase Process (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Benchmarking is the continuous and systematic process of measuring an organization's products, services, and processes against those of best-in-class performers — either within the same industry or across industries — to identify performance gaps and implement improvements.

**Robert C. Camp's 5-Phase Model (6 Marks):**

| **Phase** | **Key Activity** | **Output** |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Plan** | Identify the process to benchmark; define metrics; choose partner | Benchmarking plan, metric KPIs |
| **2. Analyze** | Collect As-Is data on own process; collect partner data; compute performance gap | Gap report |
| **3. Integrate** | Present findings; gain leadership acceptance; set improvement targets | Target setting & leadership buy-in |
| **4. Action** | Develop implementation plan; pilot changes; monitor | Process improvement & project plan |
| **5. Maturity** | Embed benchmarking as culture; refine practices; become best-in-class | Continuous improvement loop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Point for KTU:** Phase 5 ensures that benchmarking is not a **one-time project** but a **continuous cycle** — emphasizing the "Maturity" phase makes the answer stand out.

**(b) Service Sector & IT Sector Applications (7 Marks):**

**Service Sector Applications (3.5 Marks):**
- **Hospitals:** Benchmarking average Emergency Room wait time against top hospitals (e.g., Apollo vs Max).
- **Airlines:** On-time performance, baggage handling, in-flight service comparison.
- **Banking:** Account opening time, loan processing turnaround, ATM uptime.
- **Hospitality:** Check-in time, room turnover time, guest satisfaction scores.

**IT Sector Applications (3.5 Marks):**
- **Help Desk / IT Support:** Average ticket resolution time, first-call resolution rate.
- **Software Development:** Defect density (defects per KLOC), sprint velocity, code review coverage.
- **Cloud Services:** SLA uptime (99.9% vs 99.99%), latency benchmarks.
- **IT Service Management:** Incident response time, MTTR, change success rate vs industry (using ITIL/COBIT benchmarks).
- **Example:** Infosys benchmarking its DevOps deployment frequency against global peers.

**Conclusion:** Benchmarking is a **sector-agnostic** improvement lever, but its metrics must be **tailored** to whether the process is physical (manufacturing), experiential (service), or digital (IT).

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Variant]**

**(a)** What is **FMEA**? Explain its **types** and list the **stages of conducting an FMEA**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** using the **6M framework**. Use a suitable example of your choice. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) FMEA – Types & Stages (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) is a systematic, team-based, proactive technique used to identify potential failures in a product or process, evaluate their S/O/D, compute RPN, and prioritize corrective actions *before* failures reach the customer.

**Types of FMEA (3 Marks):**

| **Type** | **Scope** | **When Used** |
|---|---|---|
| **System FMEA** | Entire system / subsystem | Concept / architectural design phase |
| **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** | Product design features | Detailed design phase |
| **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** | Manufacturing / assembly process | Process planning / production phase |

**Stages of FMEA (3 Marks):**

1. Assemble a **cross-functional team** (Design, Production, Quality, Service, Customer).
2. **Define scope** — boundaries, function, customer use.
3. **Identify failure modes** for each function (e.g., "Bearing seizes").
4. **Assess** Severity, Occurrence, Detection (each 1–10).
5. **Compute RPN** $= S \times O \times D$ and rank.
6. **Prioritize** failures (RPN > 200 first; 100–200 second).
7. **Recommend corrective actions** and assign owners.
8. **Reassess RPN** after actions and document lessons learned.

**(b) Fishbone Diagram — 6M Framework (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The Fishbone Diagram (developed by Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa, 1968) is a structured brainstorming tool that categorizes the potential root causes of a problem (the "effect") into major cause categories.

**Construction Steps (2 Marks):**

1. Define the **effect** clearly and place it on the right (fish head).
2. Draw the **main spine** (long arrow) pointing to the effect.
3. Draw **6 diagonal "bones"** for the 6M categories.
4. For each category, **brainstorm specific causes** as smaller sub-branches.
5. Apply the **5-Why technique** to drill down from each cause to the **root cause**.

**The 6M Categories (2 Marks):**

- **Man:** Operator skill, training, fatigue, motivation
- **Machine:** Equipment condition, calibration, capability
- **Material:** Quality, specifications, supplier, storage
- **Method:** Procedures, sequence, work instructions
- **Measurement:** Gauges, calibration, accuracy, sampling plan
- **Mother Nature:** Temperature, humidity, dust, light, ventilation

**Example (2 Marks):**
*Effect: Increase in defective products in an injection molding unit.*
- **Man:** New operator untrained on parameter setting
- **Machine:** Barrel heater band showing intermittent failure
- **Material:** Recycled resin mixed with virgin — ratio not controlled
- **Method:** SOP for temperature profile missing
- **Measurement:** No in-process temperature log; only final visual check
- **Mother Nature:** Monsoon humidity causing resin moisture absorption

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. In Q-A, students often **omit the RPN threshold table** (100/200) — losing 1 mark.
> 2. In Q-B (Fishbone), students write only the **names** of the 6Ms but **forget to give example causes** for each — losing 2–3 marks.
> 3. In Benchmarking, students confuse **"Functional"** with **"Competitive"** benchmarking. Recall: Competitive = *same industry*; Functional = *cross-industry*.
> 4. In DOE, students frequently **omit the random run-order** step — losing 1 mark in the procedure.
> 5. **Spelling trap:** "Ishikawa" is sometimes misspelled as "Ishkawa" / "Ishikawa" — usually ignored, but in MCQs it has caught students.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist the night before your KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Benchmarking** = continuous, systematic, comparative, and action-oriented. 4 Keywords.
- [ ] **Robert C. Camp 5-Phase Model:** Plan → Analyze → Integrate → Action → Maturity. *Mnemonic: **P-A-I-A-M**.*
- [ ] **Benchmarking Types:** Internal, Competitive, Functional, Strategic, Performance. *Memorize at least 3 with examples.*
- [ ] **FMEA RPN = S × O × D.** Threshold: **< 100** Accept, **100–200** Reduce, **> 200** Mandate action.
- [ ] **FMEA Types:** System, Design (DFMEA), Process (PFMEA). Each scored S/O/D 1–10.
- [ ] **Fishbone** = 6M (Manufacturing) / 4S (Service/IT). Use 5-Why after the diagram to find root cause.
- [ ] **Lean 5 Principles (Womack & Jones):** Value, Value Stream, Flow, Pull, Perfection. *Mnemonic: **V²-F-P-P**.*
- [ ] **7 Wastes (TIMWOOD) + Skills:** Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects.
- [ ] **Lean + TQM Integration:** TQM = philosophy (Why), Lean = tools (How). Combined = Lean TQM / LSS.
- [ ] **DOE Origin:** R. A. Fisher, 1920s, UK agriculture.
- [ ] **DOE Key Terms:** Factor, Level, Response, Replicate, Main Effect, Interaction Effect.
- [ ] **DOE Designs:** Full Factorial $2^k$ (all interactions), Fractional $2^{k-p}$ (screening), Taguchi OA (robust), RSM (optimization).
- [ ] **$2^2$ design formulas:** $\beta_0 = \Sigma y / 4$, main & interaction effects = (Σ signs × y) / 4.
- [ ] **Always randomize** DOE run order to avoid time-based bias.
- [ ] **Lean-TQM-Six Sigma Sigma-Combined = Lean Six Sigma (LSS)**, the modern industry standard.
- [ ] **Benchmarking ≠ Copying.** Adapt best practices, do not plagiarize.
- [ ] **In FMEA, Detection score:** *Lower = Better* (easy to detect); *Higher = Worse* (hard to detect).
- [ ] **Service benchmarking examples** to memorize: Hospital wait time, Airline OTD, IT Help-desk MTTR.
- [ ] **Fishbone = Ishikawa = Cause-and-Effect** — all three names are interchangeable in KTU exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark comparison question (TQM vs Lean, TQM vs Six Sigma, Benchmarking vs TQM), always end with a **small table** summarizing the comparison AND a concluding line on **integration/synergy**. Examiners explicitly award the last 1–2 marks for a structured conclusion.

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Statistical Process Control (SPC) and Control Charts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** is the application of statistical techniques to monitor and control a process, ensuring it operates at its full potential to produce conforming product. A **Control Chart** is the primary tool of SPC — a time-series graphical plot of process data against statistically determined upper and lower control limits.

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
A **Control Chart** is a graphical display of a quality characteristic measured over time, plotted against an upper control limit (**UCL**), a centerline (**CL**), and a lower control limit (**LCL**). It distinguishes between **common cause variation** (inherent to the process) and **special cause variation** (due to external assignable factors). Dr. Walter Shewhart of Bell Labs introduced this concept in **1924**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Thermometer of a Process"
Imagine a doctor monitoring a patient's temperature every hour on a chart. The patient's normal body temperature is **98.6 °F** (the centerline). The doctor draws dashed lines at **99 °F** and **98 °F** as warning zones. If the temperature stays inside the dashed lines, the patient is healthy (process in control). If a spike crosses the upper dashed line, the doctor intervenes — antibiotics, rest, etc. (special cause). The thermometer doesn't treat the patient; it just *alerts* the doctor. **A control chart is the "thermometer" of any industrial process** — it doesn't fix problems, it signals when something abnormal is happening.

> [!NOTE]
> **Western Electric Rules (Nelson Rules)**: A point outside ±3σ triggers a search for special causes. Other rules include 2 of 3 consecutive points beyond ±2σ (warning), 4 of 5 beyond ±1σ, etc.

---

## 1.2 Process Capability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Capability** is the ability of a process to produce output that meets specifications, expressed as the ratio of the specification width (tolerance) to the natural process spread (**6σ**).

### Formal Definition
**Process Capability Index (Cp)** measures how well the process spread fits inside the specification limits, *assuming the process is centered*. **Cpk** measures capability when the process may be *off-center*. A **capable** process has **Cp ≥ 1.33** (industry standard).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Garage and Car" Analogy
Imagine your car's width is **6 feet** and your garage opening is **10 feet**. You can park comfortably because the garage is wider than the car. But if you drive at an angle and only **1 foot** of clearance remains on the left, you may scrape the wall. The garage size is the **tolerance (USL–LSL)**, the car width is the **process spread (6σ)**, and how centered you drive is the **Cpk**. A high **Cp** means the garage is big enough; a high **Cpk** means you also drive down the middle.

---

## 1.3 Six Sigma

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Sigma (6σ)** is a data-driven methodology and statistical measure that aims for near-perfection — **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** — by reducing process variation so that the specification limits are ±6 standard deviations from the mean. Motorola's **Bob Galvin** launched it in **1987**; **Jack Welch** made it famous at GE in **1995**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Coin Toss Tournament"
If you flipped a fair coin and needed "heads" 6 times in a row, you'd fail often. Six Sigma is like needing **six perfect outcomes in a row** before a defect appears. With 3.4 DPMO, if you produced **1 million products daily**, only about 3 to 4 would be defective — equivalent to mailing **first-class mail** for centuries with only a *single* lost letter.

---

## 1.4 Quality Function Deployment (QFD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a structured planning methodology that translates the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into specific, actionable engineering and manufacturing requirements. Developed in Japan at **Mitsubishi's Kobe Shipyards in 1972** by **Yoji Akao**. The primary tool is the **House of Quality (HOQ)** matrix.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Translator"
When a customer says *"I want a juicy, spicy burger"* (Voice of Customer), the chef translates it into: meat weight = **150 g**, grill temperature = **220 °C**, cook time = **4 min**, chili flakes = **5 g**. The **House of Quality** is the restaurant's translation chart from customer wishes to kitchen specifications.

---

## 1.5 Taguchi Quality Loss Function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taguchi Loss Function**, introduced by **Dr. Genichi Taguchi**, quantifies the **economic loss to society** caused by deviation from the target value. Unlike the traditional "within-spec = no loss" view, Taguchi argued **any deviation from target causes loss** — a continuous quadratic function.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Wedding Cake Cutting"
Traditional quality says: *"A wedding cake slice within ±5% of the ideal weight is acceptable; outside, it's defective."* Taguchi says: *"A 1 g deviation causes a tiny loss; a 4 g deviation causes 16× more loss."* Even the "acceptable" cake slice that is 3 g short disappoints the guest — there is always a loss.

---

## 1.6 Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** is a holistic maintenance philosophy, pioneered by **Seiichi Nakajima** at **Nippondenso (Toyota group) in 1971**, that aims to achieve **Zero Breakdowns, Zero Defects, Zero Accidents** through proactive and preventive maintenance involving *all employees* — from operators to top management.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Car Ownership"
Owning a family car is everyone's responsibility: Dad checks oil weekly, Mom notes tire wear, kids keep the interior clean. TPM says: operators (drivers) do daily cleaning, lubrication, and inspection; maintenance engineers handle complex repairs; management provides systems. The car (machine) runs for years without breakdowns.

---

## 1.7 TQM in Automotive Industry — Case Study Context

The **Ford Motor Company** under **Donald Petersen (President 1980–1990)** famously used the slogan *"Quality is Job 1"* and reaped record profits. **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and **Lean Six Sigma at General Electric** are the most celebrated case studies.

---

## 1.8 Industry 4.0, AI, and Ethics in Quality Management

> [!NOTE]

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Types of Control Charts

Control charts are classified based on the **type of data** (variable vs. attribute) and the **sample statistic** monitored.

### A. Control Charts for Variables (Measurable Data)

| Chart | Purpose | Monitors | Subgroup Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **X̄–R Chart** | Mean & Range | Process centering & spread | n = 2 to 10 (typically 5) |
| **X̄–S Chart** | Mean & Std. Dev. | Process centering & spread (more accurate) | n > 10 |
| **I–MR Chart (Individuals)** | Individual values & Moving Range | Continuous processes with no subgroups | n = 1 |

### B. Control Charts for Attributes (Count Data)

| Chart | Monitors | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **p-chart** | Fraction defective | Variable sample sizes |
| **np-chart** | Number of defectives | Constant sample size |
| **c-chart** | Number of defects per unit | Constant inspection unit (e.g., 100 m of fabric) |
| **u-chart** | Defects per unit | Variable sample size |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Control Chart Limits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formulas use A₂, D₃, D₄, E₂, d₂, D₉₄** constants from the standard **Shewhart Control Chart Constants Table**. Below is the master cheat sheet.

### A. X̄–R Chart Formulas (most common in KTU problems)

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$$

$$\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$

$$\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R}$$

$$\text{CL}_R = \bar{R}$$

$$\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R}$$

### B. X̄–S Chart Formulas (n > 10)

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_3 \bar{S}, \quad \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_3 \bar{S}$$

$$\text{UCL}_S = B_4 \bar{S}, \quad \text{LCL}_S = B_3 \bar{S}$$

### C. I–MR Chart (Individuals) Formulas

$$\text{UCL}_I = \bar{X} + E_2 \overline{MR}, \quad \text{LCL}_I = \bar{X} - E_2 \overline{MR}$$

$$\text{UCL}_{MR} = D_4 \overline{MR}, \quad \text{LCL}_{MR} = D_3 \overline{MR}$$

### D. p-chart Formulas

$$\text{UCL}_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$$

$$\text{CL}_p = \bar{p} = \frac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i}$$

$$\text{LCL}_p = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$$

If **LCL < 0**, set **LCL = 0** (control limits cannot be negative for fraction defective).

### E. c-chart and u-chart

$$\text{UCL}_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}, \quad \text{CL}_c = \bar{c}, \quad \text{LCL}_c = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$$

$$\text{UCL}_u = \bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}, \quad \text{LCL}_u = \bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}$$

### F. Shewhart Constants Reference Table (KTU High-Yield)

| n | A₂ | D₃ | D₄ | d₂ | D₉₄ (=3.686 for d₂) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 | 0 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.574 | 1.693 | 0 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 | 0 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 | 0 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 | 0 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 | 0.076 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of Control Charts**: Used in semiconductor wafer fabrication (SPC at TSMC, Intel), pharmaceutical batch release, automotive part machining (Toyota), and software defect tracking. They form the regulatory backbone of **ISO 9001** and **TS 16949 / IATF 16949**.

## 2.3 Process Capability — Deep Theory and Formulas

### The Capability Indices

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{\text{Tolerance Width}}{\text{Process Spread}}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left( C_{pu},\, C_{pl} \right)$$

Where:

$$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \quad C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}$$

### Related Indices

$$C_{pm} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}$$

(Taguchi index — penalizes deviation from target **T**)

$$P_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma_{\text{long}}}$$

(Process Performance — uses overall σ from full data, not within-subgroup)

$$P_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma_{\text{long}}},\, \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma_{\text{long}}} \right)$$

### Capability Interpretation Table (KTU High-Yield)

| Cp Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| Cp < 1.00 | **Incapable** — process spread exceeds tolerance |
| 1.00 ≤ Cp < 1.33 | **Marginal** — needs improvement |
| 1.33 ≤ Cp < 1.67 | **Capable** — industry minimum acceptable |
| Cp ≥ 1.67 | **Highly capable** — Six Sigma-class |

| Cpk Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| Cpk < 1.00 | Process is producing defects (off-center) |
| 1.00 ≤ Cpk < 1.33 | Marginal |
| Cpk ≥ 1.33 | Acceptable |
| Cpk ≥ 1.67 | Excellent |

## 2.4 Six Sigma — Formulas and Methodology

### Core Six Sigma Equations

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$$

$$\text{Yield} = 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6}$$

$$\text{Defect Rate} = 1 - \text{Yield}$$

### Six Sigma with 1.5σ Shift

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Six Sigma in practice uses a **1.5σ shift** because processes drift over time. So a true 6σ process has a **short-term capability of 6σ** but a **long-term performance of 4.5σ** ⇒ **3.4 DPMO**.

$$\text{DPMO at } k\sigma \text{ (with 1.5σ shift)} \leftrightarrow \text{Yield}$$

### DMAIC Roadmap (KTU High-Yield)

| Phase | Purpose | Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **D**efine | Problem statement, customer CTQs | Project charter, SIPOC, VOC |
| **M**easure | Baseline data, process capability | Control charts, MSA, Gage R&R |
| **A**nalyze | Root cause identification | Pareto, Fishbone, 5-Why, Regression |
| **I**mprove | Pilot solutions, validate | DOE, Taguchi methods, Pilot runs |
| **C**ontrol | Sustain gains | SPC, SOPs, Control plans |

## 2.5 Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — House of Quality Structure

The **House of Quality** matrix has 6 main sections:

1. **Customer Requirements (WHATs)** — left side (Voice of Customer)
2. **Technical Requirements (HOWs)** — top/ceiling
3. **Relationship Matrix** — center (Strong = 9, Moderate = 3, Weak = 1)
4. **Correlation Matrix (Roof)** — top triangular matrix showing inter-relationships among HOWs (++ positive, – – negative, × conflict)
5. **Technical Importance Ratings** — right side (computed)
6. **Competitive Assessment** — bottom rows (benchmark vs. competitors)

### Importance Weighting Formula (KTU High-Yield)

$$\text{Tech. Importance}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Customer Importance}_i \times \text{Relationship}_{ij})$$

$$\text{Relative Weight}_j = \frac{\text{Tech. Importance}_j}{\sum \text{Tech. Importance}} \times 100\%$$

## 2.6 Taguchi Loss Function — Formulas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The loss function is **quadratic** — loss grows as the **square of deviation from target**.

### Standard Taguchi Loss Function

$$L(y) = k(y - m)^2$$

Where:
- **L(y)** = Loss incurred when quality characteristic takes value y
- **y** = Actual measured value of quality characteristic
- **m** = Target value (ideal)
- **k** = Quality loss coefficient (constant)

### Determining k from Specification Limits

If the loss at the specification limit (±Δ₀ from target) is **A₀** (cost of replacing/repairing one defective unit):

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$$

### Engineering Signal-to-Noise Ratio (η) — Taguchi's Robust Design

For **smaller-the-better** characteristics:

$$\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2 \right)$$

For **larger-the-better** characteristics:

$$\eta = -10 \log_{10}\left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{y_i^2} \right)$$

For **nominal-the-best** characteristics:

$$\eta = 10 \log_{10}\left( \frac{\mu^2}{\sigma^2} \right)$$

### Engineering Utility
Taguchi's loss function is used in automotive tolerance design (Ford, Toyota), semiconductor manufacturing, and pharmaceutical formulations. It justifies tighter tolerances by quantifying **total societal cost** (warranty, returns, customer dissatisfaction).

$$\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$$
